speaker: interviewer
text: --of an interview with Simon Stemer on November 15-- November 13, 1995. You were telling me about having lunch at the-- when you were at [? Statmeier ?] [? Shoe ?].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When we saw the door open up, and the gate-- this was over there a gate-- and this coming in a Ger-- a Gestapo, and some Ukrainian police. I said to the-- my friend, maybe we will run away. They said there's no time to run away. They was over there. And I ran to a-- in a little wood. It was over there, not far. And I was-- I was over there about two, three hours. This was about 12 o'clock. Well, I walked back where the place is-- but-- but this place was over there a home, not far, some private people was it. I went in this place. And they have over there-- they bring in wood from the-- with the horses and the wagons. I help this to take out the wood, to put it together, to take it apart. It was over there a Gentile-- a Gentile what he worked over there, a carpenter. He tell me-- he told me, hide it. If not, you get killed. Because he saw the Gestapo with this-- they come over there to look again for people. Maybe somebody saw me over there-- they come.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in, in a shed. There was a couple cars over there. And I was hiding. I was over there sitting till it gets dark. When it get dark, I come out and I sit down over there, over at the bench. And the lady come out. I ask her, maybe you can give me a little bit water? I don't have water. I don't eat a whole day. She bring me out a little bit water and a piece of bread. I ate it up. And I'm sitting on the bench. Her husband was working in the city hall, the [NON-ENGLISH]. He come home [INAUDIBLE] at about 9 o'clock. He saw me. He said, now you can go home. It's quiet. I come home. Nobody home. The Ukrainian police is running around and grabbing the people and-- and taking what they want. They ask me, what are you doing here? I just come from work. Because I'm supposed to be already dead, because they took the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: This day, this Saturday, they took 460 people. They grabbed mens-- young, old-- and loaded up in the trucks. What happened with this nobody knows. One said is killed-- they killed them. One said, no. Only they-- the-- the Polish people over there, where they live, they was anti-Semite. They come always and tell stories, oh, I was in Lublin, I-- to the ladies. I saw your husband. The [INAUDIBLE] is coming. He said to the Judenrate, I saw the people. They're working in the other side. This was everything lie. This was in Friday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. This was-- they took these people. Later, we find out they dig holes in the woods and they killed them over there. We start to be memories already, even-- the people got killed. Meantime, the widows with the children-- they went to the Judenrate for help. The Judenrate went to the [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] said, we will send the woman with the kids to-- to the-- over there, where the men are working. They was-- they was standing in a line to register. Meantime, they had to get some help. Meantime, they hear as they want to send them is the line ran away. And everybody, the [NON-ENGLISH], said, everybody can take packages till 60 pound and we go. This was Friday. They was then 18 of September, 1941. Like there come this guy Lahotka. Yeah, and a couple days later it was again a new Judenrate. They just left the old Judenrate president. And the other one was taken with. And this president-- they find out some other people and they make another committee, a Judenrate. And they [INAUDIBLE] the people what they got left-- they worked by this-- by the bricks, what they make the sidewalk. And the Judenrate give for these people what they want to eat lunch. The Judenrate have lunch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday, the 18 of September, is the guy-- is a guy from the police come, Lahotka, and he take-- and he took-- said he need 10 people for work. They picked up 10 young people. We went over there. We come over there. They-- he ask, who is an engineer? Nobody answered. Later, he-- he on-- what the [NON-ENGLISH], the police was over there. It was not far from the woods. He wanted to clean up, to make a bright way to go to the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walk over there. We cleaned up, all around, and make it-- this. About 5 o'clock, we go for lunch. When it becomes lunch, is the-- going back from lunch, I see two trucks with the German. So I-- maybe they're passing by. We work till 5 o'clock. 5 o'clock, a police comes out with a gun, with ammunition, and he start to [INAUDIBLE]. It was cleaned up over there. It was over there a woods. He gave out saws to cut the woods-- just to cut. We cut the woods as it got dark. It got dark, he took us where to go. They put us in, in a basement. It was a little bit window open, over there in the basement. And we said, yeah. And we put it, in this basement, was two benches. There also over there was working two tailors. They bring him in, too. There was already 12 people. When we come in this room, we say, maybe you will give us a little bit water after a day work. He said, you don't need more water. And he locked us up. Later, we hear the doors opening up. The Ukrainian police come in and take away-- somebody got a sweater, a good jacket, they took it away. And we sitting over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple hours later, again open up-- comes in another Ukrainian-- two Ukrainians. So they look. There was already nothing to take, because the other was taken. We were sitting over there. We know we will be killed very soon. We were sitting and crying and howling, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we say, [NON-ENGLISH]. I say it, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we was prepared to get killed. We just was begging, God, if we have to get killed, why we have to keep us here? Kill us right away. We know we dead, no question about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we sitting in this basement. We have a little window over there. It was open. We hear they said in Polish, [POLISH]. Boys, get ready. Take the shovels. We know they go dig the holes for us. We was praying a whole night. Nobody was sleeping, just what faster to get out, killed. At 6 o'clock in the morning, we hear it's got life, people running around. We saw some German. We saw lakes. We don't saw the people. No, we-- we understand already they getting prepared for us to get killed. We're sitting over there till about 11 o'clock, 10 o'clock maybe. The doors opening up. There come a cart in the hallway. And we wait-- we staying in the hallway. We looked out from the hallway. It's-- in one side, is the Ukrainian police, in the other side the Gestapo. In the middle is already going the people what they live in Kostopol-- young and old, they already start to stray them out to go. And-- and we hear hollering, crying. And they-- what they have everything, they put away in  the side. And we staying in the hallway. The [NON-ENGLISH] come over and with a Ukrainian police. And he said, 12 people, young people-- he said young people they need to walk, to walk by the-- by the street, what we was doing but it-- we went in.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 11 o'clock, we hear the shooting. They start to shoot the people what this was-- young, old. We sitting. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] But and the door was already opened. We was not locked up. The door was open. Where you will go? You know there [INAUDIBLE] of Ukrainian police over there. You're not-- one Ukrainian police come in. And he said, be quiet. You will go home alive. You will go home alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're sitting. It was already about 5 o'clock. We went out. We see this policeman. He said, tell them we don't eat nothing. Give us something to eat. He bring us in two loaves of bread. And this was over there a pump of water. We took a pail with the-- with the water. And we eat up and we go back in the-- we're sitting in the basement till it's get dark already. We walked up. We went to the office. It's over there. We saw the guy, this guy Lahotka.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are you doing here? He ask us. He was sure we killed already. We told him we was in the basement. And was the [NON-ENGLISH] said young people who need to work. OK, he said. Go home. I come home. We come home-- nobody in. Go from one house to the other house. You don't see nobody. This was Saturday night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, listen, I have-- I took something and I lay down. The morning, I go out. Later, they start to come some people to ghetto. My boss come back with his family. He have three or four kids with a wife. And-- and some of the other people come back. And it start to get together some people. This was in that time between Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur in 1941. We was over there-- I wouldn't say-- it was not taking too long. One man, what he was a printer-- he print the paper-- they got him together with this transport to go to the wood. Only the paper need him for the printer. They took him out. And he told us the story of what they did. When the people come, they went in, in a basement. They put away everything what they had. And they went out naked. And they went to the woods. He saw this and he told us what has happened. This was in-- in a-- between Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. The Kol Nidre, we get together. This was maybe 15 or 20 people-- mens, and woman, and kids. And we say Kol Nidre. And we decided tomorrow to [NON-ENGLISH]. They will come together, because the people will have to go to work. Some people there was working. To [NON-ENGLISH], he come back, this printer. He was with us. He said the [NON-ENGLISH] said he will make from the shul a ghetto. And the people where they now will live in the ghetto. I said-- I was thinking, and now it's bad. If I have to live in the ghetto it will be bad. Early in the morning, not far from over there, I know was a Jewish farmer. I know him. And so I went into this Jewish farmer. I come to the Jewish farmer. He was afraid to hold me in the house. He was already [? sick. ?] He keep me in the-- in the shed. And he bring me to eat. Two days I was over there in the shed. And he bring me food to eat. He was afraid. I told him, maybe you can get me a [NON-ENGLISH] to go to Rovno. It was over there 30 kilometers. I said, here, I have a watch. Maybe you give somebody the watch to give me a [NON-ENGLISH]. The other day, he come back and he bring me a [NON-ENGLISH]. I give him the watch. Only it's no stamps, because it was new. As-- as I went out, and I walk, I took off my-- we have the [NON-ENGLISH] one in the front, a round one, four inches round, yellow. I took it off. And I went to Rovno. I come in to Rovno. You know, like in the old towns, people were sitting in the front, by the doors and talking over. I come over there. And I told the people I come from Kostopol. And I told them what happened in Kostopol. This was in Rovno. They was not believing me. They was thinking I'm crazy. Only, they took me into the house and they gave me something to eat and I slept over. In the morning, I decide to go to the Judenrate. Maybe they will give me a [NON-ENGLISH] to go home. The-- I know what happened in Kostopol. I was thinking maybe over there is not so bad, like here. I come Sunday in the Judenrate. And I told them I want a [NON-ENGLISH]. And I told them what has happened in Kostopol. They was not believing. They told me to come tomorrow, Monday. Come Monday, this Monday is already the German come to the Judenrate this day and said they need warm clothes, good heavy coats, the [INAUDIBLE]-- anything, even pillows. They need to be-- because they was going already by the-- going to Russia, and was already cool over there. They need something. Was already in Judenrate nobody to talk. Because I was over there. I went back to this people what I was over there. And I was over there about a couple days. And I don't know what to do. I was thinking to go back home. Because I have to go back to Lutsk. And from Lutsk to go to Wlodzimierz it was 150 kilometer, about 120 miles. And so I decided to go in the night. And so I went two nights-- I went, I come into Lutsk. My sister, she was working in a hospital. Only it was over there a lot of people took away. They take away the people to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was over there with my sister a day. She went back to work. And I went to go to [? Ludma. ?] And I walk on the night and I come into [? Ludma. ?] When I come into [? Ludma, ?] it was a different life over there. People were standing, selling apples with different kind of a fruit stand. I have over there a cousin, a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] is? He is writing the Torah, the [NON-ENGLISH]. His name was Chaim. I go in and tell him the story. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They don't believe me either. I told the [? Ludma ?] the story. They don't believe me either. How they can take people and to kill them, just like this, for nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was working in a mill, where they make flour-- a mill. As he took me over there, I was over there a little while. And he took me over there. And I was working over there too, in the mill. When you work in a mill, you're not short in a little bit flour you bring home, or some-- some kind of barley or something. I brought it home and I give him sometimes. I tell him-- he just bring me-- he got some news. The German got a bad drop here. And the German-- they falling apart, the German. And I was working in the mill. This was already in 1942. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, I go out in the street, when I come back from Kostopol, in [? Ludma, ?] and I bro-- bought five or six pound big apples. My sister [? Laura ?] bring home the apples she has-- [? Chaya, ?] what are you doing? What-- you spent so much money for this apple. I said, now I am living, I will eat a good apple. Because I will not live anyway. Because I-- all right. Later, a couple months later, they start to talk about a ghetto-- make a ghetto. I go into this [NON-ENGLISH], to my cousin, Chaim. And [INAUDIBLE] ask him, Chaim, what do you say now? You see? I told you what this was and-- ay, you see these little thin Poles with the wires? They will stay four, six weeks. And they will-- and he bring-- always, everyday he got news the German is getting [INAUDIBLE]. OK, what can you do? I know it's not possible to exist. This was all around kill the people. They [NON-ENGLISH] told this Judenrate in [? Ludma, ?] you are good people. You will live. Nothing will happen to your town. I-- I was not believing. Only, he said so, he said so. We work over there till-- till [INAUDIBLE], 17 days in [INAUDIBLE] [? Hillel. ?] And later, they make the ghetto. They make the ghetto in [? Ludma. ?] And people was going to work. If-- if a policeman saw you in the street and he was thinking maybe you carrying home something, he shot you right in the place. Because usually, you go out, you take a shirt, you take a jacket, you give it to a Gentile, he'll give you a chicken, or give you some bread, give you some butter. And if a policeman was thinking you carry something and he find you, he shot you right in the place. And nobody can do something there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- this was going like this, in the ghetto. I remember 17 days in [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], before Rosh Hashanah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now the [INAUDIBLE] when we worked in the mill is the [NON-ENGLISH] give an order, he come to the Judenrate-- the blueprint-- and he said, over there, six kilometer from [? Ludma, ?] is big woods. And we have to bring tanks here. We have to make-- dig holes to come hide the tanks. And he give out a plan. We need four big holes, 30 feet long-- 30 feet-- 40 feet wide, and two and a half feet deep. And in the side, the sand what you take out will be left in the side, in the tree side, and one way to go in. Yeah, is-- is before this, they said, all the people what they work for the government or for the [INAUDIBLE]-- they have to go back to-- to the ghetto. They have interest work for the people they have to do for the government. It's all of the people what they work-- they don't go out more to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I come back from Kostopol, and I have over there a [NON-ENGLISH]. Oh, OK.
